response:
for each index i in the array nums we need to compute the sum of absolute differences between nums[i] and all other elements in the array except itself if i is not a multiple of 3. however if i is a multiple of 3 we calculate the sum of squares of the differences instead. since the array is sorted we can leverage this property to compute the sum efficiently. for the absolute differences we can use prefix sums to calculate the sum of elements before and after the current index and derive the total sum in o(1) time per index. for the squares we need to compute the sum of squares of differences which can be done using the formula sum((nums[i] - nums[j])^2) for all j. this can be simplified using the sum of squares and the square of the sum.

***

to implement this we first precompute prefix sums and prefix squares. then for each index i we check if it is a multiple of 3. if it is we calculate the sum of squares using the precomputed values. otherwise we use the prefix sums to calculate the absolute difference sum. this ensures the solution runs in linear time which is essential given the constraints. the key is to handle the two different cases efficiently and ensure that the array is processed correctly. by using the sorted property and precomputing necessary values we can achieve the desired result without exceeding time limits. the algorithm ensures that all elements are processed in o(n) time and the result array is built correctly.
